EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One category improvements were noted in drought severity in areas that had previously been in Extreme(D3) or even some Severe(D2) drought.  By the end of the month, there were no areas in southern New Mexico in Extreme(D3) drought or worse.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was no change in drought conditions through the month of January.  The western half of the zone remained in Severe(D2) drought and the east in Moderate(D1) drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough with an associated cold front moved through during the early morning hours of January 8th.  Wind gusts up to 73 mph were reported on the eastern slopes of the Organ Mountains on the White Sands Missile Range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak gust of 73 mph was recorded at the White Sands Missile Range Building 1830.  Other gusts around the area included 72 mph at San Augustin Pass, 68 mph at the Main Post and 63 mph at the Emre Mesonet site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A classic winter weather pattern setup across the region with an upper low moving through northwest Mexico while a back door cold front moved into Otero County. Southwest flow aloft ahead of the upper low kept some warm air aloft over the area and the Sacramento Mountains experienced a period of freezing rain before transitioning over to all snow.  This combination of winter weather types caused numerous power outages and over 2 feet of snow in the Cloudcroft area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow and ice caused widespread power outages.  As of 10 AM MST on Jan 24th, approximately 5800 people were without power in Otero County, most in the Sacramento Mountains.  The Otero County Electric COOP was still making repairs over 2 days later with reports of a lot of tree damage and broken poles due to ice loading.  ||Total snowfall amounts across the zone included 20 to 26 inches in the Cloudcroft area and 6.5 inches about 6 miles northwest of Mayhill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two back to back winter storm systems impacted northern and central NM with the first from late January 7th through the morning of January 8th with the second colder system crossing the state late January 8th through the afternoon of January 9th. The first storm brought snow to mainly the mountains and nearby highlands, with valley and plains locations receiving mainly rain. Gusty west winds were observed across the central highlands midday on January 8th between the two storm systems with a peak wind gust of 65 mph at a Ziamet station southeast of Vaughn. The second storm system approached the state during the afternoon hours of January 8th with western New Mexico impacted by a band of snow on the leading edge of the storm. This band of snow moved into the middle Rio Grande Valley during the evening. This band contained a convective storm with large hail and graupel across the Albuquerque metro area. Accumulating graupel and hail forced the closure of I-40 west of Albuquerque with one inch diameter hail and graupel observed at the National Weather Service office. As the core of the system moved into western NM the night of January 8th and early morning of January 9th, a wraparound snow band brought moderate to heavy snow across northwest and north-central NM, including Farmington, Taos, and Santa Fe. Moderate to heavy snow then shifted to northeast NM on the north side of the storm during the morning and midday hours of January 9th. North wind gusts of up to 45 mph resulted in blowing snow and near whiteout conditions and the temporary closures of I-25 at Raton Pass and U.S. Highway 64/87 between Raton and Clayton. Snow across northeast NM tapered off during the early evening hours as the system exited into the central Great Plains. A lingering band of snow across Espanola, Los Alamos, Santa Fe, and Glorieta brought some light snow during the evening hours of January 9th before skies finally cleared up and temperatures plummeted overnight.||In the end, snowfall amounts of 2 to 6 inches were observed across lower elevations of northwest and north central New Mexico, 4 to 7 inches across the Jemez and Tusas Mountains with an estimated 10 inches at the Bateman SNOTEL site in the southern Tusas Mountains. The Sangre de Cristo Mountains observed 1 to 2 feet of snow with the higher end of this range observed at Ski Santa Fe and the Santa Fe SNOTEL site. The eastern foothills of the Albuquerque Metro observed 1 to 2 inches of a snow and graupel mix and the Sandia and Manzano Mountains observing around 5 to 9 inches of snow with locally a foot on the Sandia Crest. The west-central and southwest mountains observed around 3 to 5 inches of snow with locally up to an estimated 16 inches at Whitewater Baldy Peak. Finally, the Ruidoso area observed around 1 to 4 inches of snow with 10 inches at Sierra Blanca Peak and Ski Apache.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHS observer in Miami, NM observed an average snowfall of 6 inches and a CoCoRaHS observer in Raton observed an average snowfall of 5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front caused a brief period of high sustained winds at Carlsbad Caverns National Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Texas Mesonet station 164 Carlsbad Caverns NP measured intermittent sustained winds between 40-42 mph following the passage of a front.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong arctic cold front moved across west Texas and southeast New Mexico during the afternoon and evening hours of the 23rd. Meanwhile, a potent upper-level trough neared the region from the west. Widespread rain began during the morning of the 23rd in western portions of the area and gradually transitioned eastward through the day. As the front moved across the area, rain transitioned to sleet and snow from north to south (with freezing rain being more common south of the Pecos River and in the Davis Mountains). Off and on bands of sleet and snow continued across the Permian Basin and southeast New Mexico on the 24th, and became mostly snow by the 25th before moving east of the area late in the afternoon. Snowfall/sleet totals reached winter storm criteria across much of the northern basin and in southeast New Mexico. Lower snow and ice totals were reported further south.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A newspaper reported a two-day total of 3.8 inches of sleet/snow near Hobbs. The total duration of the event is estimated from radar and nearby AWOS reports.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning of the 8th, a shortwave and upper-level jet max began to move over west Texas and southeast New Mexico while a surface cyclone strengthened over the Oklahoma Panhandle. As the jet max moved overhead, very strong to high winds developed in the higher terrain (namely the Guadalupe and Davis Mountains), as well as the surrounding foothills. Gusts up to 80-90 mph were observed in the Guadalupe Mountains and at Carlsbad Caverns National Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Texas Mesonet station 164 Carlsbad Caverns NP measured numerous high wind gusts, including a gust to 90 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant winter storm impacted northern and central NM from January 23rd through January 27th, with a combination of heavy snow, ice, and extreme cold impacts. A subtropical upper low beginning off the coast of the northern Baja Peninsula transported abundant moisture into the Desert Southwest and New Mexico in particular, much of it overriding an anomalously bitter cold and shallow Arctic airmass over eastern NM. This warmer moisture laden air overriding subfreezing surface temperatures set the stage for freezing rain and sleet for areas along the south-central highlands to the south-central mountains and southeastern plains of NM January 23rd through January 24th. Slick icy travel conditions were reported in these areas alongside ice and snow downing limbs and trees across the Ruidoso area burn scars. Power outages and road closures in the Ruidoso area lasted multiple days from January 24th to at least January 28th. While the entire event was snow for the northeastern plains and northern mountains, the transition from rain to snow through the middle Rio Grande Valley and an icy wintry mix to all snow through the aforementioned areas of ice did not occur until late January 25th when a trailing upper level system over the Four Corners area brought snow levels down to valley floors. The highest snow accumulations of 30 plus inches fell atop Sierra Blanca Peak just west of Ruidoso. A Village Disaster Declaration was made January 27th. General amounts of 2 to 8 inches of snow fell elsewhere across the northern mountains, highlands, and southeastern plains. Extreme cold settled into eastern NM behind the Arctic airmass where 72 to 96 hours of subfreezing temperatures lasted in many areas. Freezing fog limiting visibility in these areas were present during the first 48 hours of the event, with extreme cold wind chills falling below zero to negative 10 to 15 degrees below zero during the nights thereafter through the morning of January 27th. Conditions from ice and snow began improving January 26th while the extreme cold and wind chills persisted for the southeast plains particularly near Roswell through January 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter a few miles south of Dunken measured 4 inches of snowfall.

At its peak, 8,551 customers were without power. Power was restored to all customers by the morning of January 28th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds associated with a weakening upper level system crossed New Mexico through the day January 2nd, 2026. Subsidence behind the front combined with some terrain interaction helped bring these winds to the surface to a few isolated locations of central NM, particularly western Guadalupe County. Peak winds surpassed 67 mph at a ZiaMet location along U.S. Highway 285 southeast of Vaughn. No damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The mesonet station southeast of Vaughn recorded a gust that surpassed the instrumentation rating of 67 mph, while sustained winds regularly surpassed 40 mph. The Thompson Cattle Ranch mesonet station also measured a wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant winter storm impacted northern and central NM from January 23rd through January 27th, with a combination of heavy snow, ice, and extreme cold impacts. A subtropical upper low beginning off the coast of the northern Baja Peninsula transported abundant moisture into the Desert Southwest and New Mexico in particular, much of it overriding an anomalously bitter cold and shallow Arctic airmass over eastern NM. This warmer moisture laden air overriding subfreezing surface temperatures set the stage for freezing rain and sleet for areas along the south-central highlands to the south-central mountains and southeastern plains of NM January 23rd through January 24th. Slick icy travel conditions were reported in these areas alongside ice and snow downing limbs and trees across the Ruidoso area burn scars. Power outages and road closures in the Ruidoso area lasted multiple days from January 24th to at least January 28th. While the entire event was snow for the northeastern plains and northern mountains, the transition from rain to snow through the middle Rio Grande Valley and an icy wintry mix to all snow through the aforementioned areas of ice did not occur until late January 25th when a trailing upper level system over the Four Corners area brought snow levels down to valley floors. The highest snow accumulations of 30 plus inches fell atop Sierra Blanca Peak just west of Ruidoso. A Village Disaster Declaration was made January 27th. General amounts of 2 to 8 inches of snow fell elsewhere across the northern mountains, highlands, and southeastern plains. Extreme cold settled into eastern NM behind the Arctic airmass where 72 to 96 hours of subfreezing temperatures lasted in many areas. Freezing fog limiting visibility in these areas were present during the first 48 hours of the event, with extreme cold wind chills falling below zero to negative 10 to 15 degrees below zero during the nights thereafter through the morning of January 27th. Conditions from ice and snow began improving January 26th while the extreme cold and wind chills persisted for the southeast plains particularly near Roswell through January 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Vacas Locas SNOTEL observed an estimated 10 inches of snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough moved east across the northern Rockies during the day. Strong mid and upper level winds over eastern New Mexico at the base of this trough along with deep daytime mixing resulted in brief high wind gusts at Bartley, Clines Corners, and the Tucumcari Airport. Winds tapered off during the mid evening hours as the disturbance exited east into the central United States.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Tucumcari Municipal Airport ASOS measured a brief high wind gust of 58 mph.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough moved east across the northern Rockies during the day. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two back to back winter storm systems impacted northern and central NM with the first from late January 7th through the morning of January 8th with the second colder system crossing the state late January 8th through the afternoon of January 9th. The first storm brought snow to mainly the mountains and nearby highlands, with valley and plains locations receiving mainly rain. Gusty west winds were observed across the central highlands midday on January 8th between the two storm systems with a peak wind gust of 65 mph at a Ziamet station southeast of Vaughn. The second storm system approached the state during the afternoon hours of January 8th with western New Mexico impacted by a band of snow on the leading edge of the storm. This band of snow moved into the middle Rio Grande Valley during the evening. This band contained a convective storm with large hail and graupel across the Albuquerque metro area. Accumulating graupel and hail forced the closure of I-40 west of Albuquerque with one inch diameter hail and graupel observed at the National Weather Service office. As the core of the system moved into western NM the night of January 8th and early morning of January 9th, a wraparound snow band brought moderate to heavy snow across northwest and north-central NM, including Farmington, Taos, and Santa Fe. Moderate to heavy snow then shifted to northeast NM on the north side of the storm during the morning and midday hours of January 9th. North wind gusts of up to 45 mph resulted in blowing snow and near whiteout conditions and the temporary closures of I-25 at Raton Pass and U.S. Highway 64/87 between Raton and Clayton. Snow across northeast NM tapered off during the early evening hours as the system exited into the central Great Plains. A lingering band of snow across Espanola, Los Alamos, Santa Fe, and Glorieta brought some light snow during the evening hours of January 9th before skies finally cleared up and temperatures plummeted overnight.||In the end, snowfall amounts of 2 to 6 inches were observed across lower elevations of northwest and north central New Mexico, 4 to 7 inches across the Jemez and Tusas Mountains with an estimated 10 inches at the Bateman SNOTEL site in the southern Tusas Mountains. The Sangre de Cristo Mountains observed 1 to 2 feet of snow with the higher end of this range observed at Ski Santa Fe and the Santa Fe SNOTEL site. The eastern foothills of the Albuquerque Metro observed 1 to 2 inches of a snow and graupel mix and the Sandia and Manzano Mountains observing around 5 to 9 inches of snow with locally a foot on the Sandia Crest. The west-central and southwest mountains observed around 3 to 5 inches of snow with locally up to an estimated 16 inches at Whitewater Baldy Peak. Finally, the Ruidoso area observed around 1 to 4 inches of snow with 10 inches at Sierra Blanca Peak and Ski Apache.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail/graupel observed at the National Weather Service office.

General amounts of 2 to 8 inches of snow fell elsewhere across the northern mountains, highlands, and southeastern plains. Extreme cold settled into eastern NM behind the Arctic airmass where 72 to 96 hours of subfreezing temperatures lasted in many areas. Freezing fog limiting visibility in these areas were present during the first 48 hours of the event, with extreme cold wind chills falling below zero to negative 10 to 15 degrees below zero during the nights thereafter through the morning of January 27th. Conditions from ice and snow began improving January 26th while the extreme cold and wind chills persisted for the southeast plains particularly near Roswell through January 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sandia Ski observed 8 inches of snowfall. Elsewhere across the zone, CoCoRaHS observers near Sedillo and Edgewood measured 4 to 5 inches of snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant winter storm impacted northern and central NM from January 23rd through January 27th, with a combination of heavy snow, ice, and extreme cold impacts. A subtropical upper low beginning off the coast of the northern Baja Peninsula transported abundant moisture into the Desert Southwest and New Mexico in particular, much of it overriding an anomalously bitter cold and shallow Arctic airmass over eastern NM. This warmer moisture laden air overriding subfreezing surface temperatures set the stage for freezing rain and sleet for areas along the south-central highlands to the south-central mountains and southeastern plains of NM January 23rd through January 24th. Slick icy travel conditions were reported in these areas alongside ice and snow downing limbs and trees across the Ruidoso area burn scars. Power outages and road closures in the Ruidoso area lasted multiple days from January 24th to at least January 28th. While the entire event was snow for the northeastern plains and northern mountains, the transition from rain to snow through the middle Rio Grande Valley and an icy wintry mix to all snow through the aforementioned areas of ice did not occur until late January 25th when a trailing upper level system over the Four Corners area brought snow levels down to valley floors. Conditions from ice and snow began improving January 26th while the extreme cold and wind chills persisted for the southeast plains particularly near Roswell through January 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHS observer a few miles west of Hagerman measured 0.3 inches of ice accretion from freezing rain late January 23rd into the morning of January 24th. This was followed with 8.8 inches of snowfall midday January 24th into the late morning hours of January 25th. Other snowfall reports across the zone midday January 24th through the late morning hours of January 25th include 8 inches near the Roswell Airport, 6.5 inches in Lake Arthur, and 5 to 6 inches on the north side of Roswell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two back to back winter storm systems impacted northern and central NM with the first from late January 7th through the morning of January 8th with the second colder system crossing the state late January 8th through the afternoon of January 9th. The first storm brought snow to mainly the mountains and nearby highlands, with valley and plains locations receiving mainly rain. Gusty west winds were observed across the central highlands midday on January 8th between the two storm systems with a peak wind gust of 65 mph at a Ziamet station southeast of Vaughn. The second storm system approached the state during the afternoon hours of January 8th with western New Mexico impacted by a band of snow on the leading edge of the storm. This band of snow moved into the middle Rio Grande Valley during the evening. This band contained a convective storm with large hail and graupel across the Albuquerque metro area. Accumulating graupel and hail forced the closure of I-40 west of Albuquerque with one inch diameter hail and graupel observed at the National Weather Service office. As the core of the system moved into western NM the night of January 8th and early morning of January 9th, a wraparound snow band brought moderate to heavy snow across northwest and north-central NM, including Farmington, Taos, and Santa Fe. Moderate to heavy snow then shifted to northeast NM on the north side of the storm during the morning and midday hours of January 9th. North wind gusts of up to 45 mph resulted in blowing snow and near whiteout conditions and the temporary closures of I-25 at Raton Pass and U.S. Highway 64/87 between Raton and Clayton. Snow across northeast NM tapered off during the early evening hours as the system exited into the central Great Plains. A lingering band of snow across Espanola, Los Alamos, Santa Fe, and Glorieta brought some light snow during the evening hours of January 9th before skies finally cleared up and temperatures plummeted overnight.||In the end, snowfall amounts of 2 to 6 inches were observed across lower elevations of northwest and north central New Mexico, 4 to 7 inches across the Jemez and Tusas Mountains with an estimated 10 inches at the Bateman SNOTEL site in the southern Tusas Mountains. The Sangre de Cristo Mountains observed 1 to 2 feet of snow with the higher end of this range observed at Ski Santa Fe and the Santa Fe SNOTEL site. The eastern foothills of the Albuquerque Metro observed 1 to 2 inches of a snow and graupel mix and the Sandia and Manzano Mountains observing around 5 to 9 inches of snow with locally a foot on the Sandia Crest. The west-central and southwest mountains observed around 3 to 5 inches of snow with locally up to an estimated 16 inches at Whitewater Baldy Peak. Finally, the Ruidoso area observed around 1 to 4 inches of snow with 10 inches at Sierra Blanca Peak and Ski Apache.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Silver Creek Divide SNOTEL observed an estimated 16 inches of snowfall. CoCoRaHS observers along U.S. Highway 60 between Quemado and Datil measured an overall 3 to 5 inches of snowfall.

The west-central and southwest mountains observed around 3 to 5 inches of snow with locally up to an estimated 16 inches at Whitewater Baldy Peak. Finally, the Ruidoso area observed around 1 to 4 inches of snow with 10 inches at Sierra Blanca Peak and Ski Apache.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Taos Ski Valley observed 13 inches of snowfall. An estimated 5 to 10 inches of snowfall was observed across the rest of the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two back to back winter storm systems impacted northern and central NM with the first from late January 7th through the morning of January 8th with the second colder system crossing the state late January 8th through the afternoon of January 9th. The first storm brought snow to mainly the mountains and nearby highlands, with valley and plains locations receiving mainly rain. Gusty west winds were observed across the central highlands midday on January 8th between the two storm systems with a peak wind gust of 65 mph at a Ziamet station southeast of Vaughn. The second storm system approached the state during the afternoon hours of January 8th with western New Mexico impacted by a band of snow on the leading edge of the storm. This band of snow moved into the middle Rio Grande Valley during the evening. This band contained a convective storm with large hail and graupel across the Albuquerque metro area. Accumulating graupel and hail forced the closure of I-40 west of Albuquerque with one inch diameter hail and graupel observed at the National Weather Service office. As the core of the system moved into western NM the night of January 8th and early morning of January 9th, a wraparound snow band brought moderate to heavy snow across northwest and north-central NM, including Farmington, Taos, and Santa Fe. Moderate to heavy snow then shifted to northeast NM on the north side of the storm during the morning and midday hours of January 9th. North wind gusts of up to 45 mph resulted in blowing snow and near whiteout conditions and the temporary closures of I-25 at Raton Pass and U.S. Highway 64/87 between Raton and Clayton. Snow across northeast NM tapered off during the early evening hours as the system exited into the central Great Plains. A lingering band of snow across Espanola, Los Alamos, Santa Fe, and Glorieta brought some light snow during the evening hours of January 9th before skies finally cleared up and temperatures plummeted overnight.||In the end, snowfall amounts of 2 to 6 inches were observed across lower elevations of northwest and north central New Mexico, 4 to 7 inches across the Jemez and Tusas Mountains with an estimated 10 inches at the Bateman SNOTEL site in the southern Tusas Mountains. The Sangre de Cristo Mountains observed 1 to 2 feet of snow with the higher end of this range observed at Ski Santa Fe and the Santa Fe SNOTEL site. The eastern foothills of the Albuquerque Metro observed 1 to 2 inches of a snow and graupel mix and the Sandia and Manzano Mountains observing around 5 to 9 inches of snow with locally a foot on the Sandia Crest. The west-central and southwest mountains observed around 3 to 5 inches of snow with locally up to an estimated 16 inches at Whitewater Baldy Peak. Finally, the Ruidoso area observed around 1 to 4 inches of snow with 10 inches at Sierra Blanca Peak and Ski Apache.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observers on the northern and eastern areas of Taos observed 8 to 9.5 inches of snowfall. CoCoRaHS observers in Ranchos de Taos observed 5.5 inches of snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: January 2026 was generally a mild and wet month for most of northern and central New Mexico. There were two active periods during the month, with the first being from January 7th to 9th and the other from January 23rd to 25th. This resulted in well above average precipitation across southern and central areas and near to slightly above average precipitation across east central and northern areas. Temperatures for the month were well above average across western and central areas and well below normal across the southeast plains. The well below normal temperatures across the southeast plains were due to a potent arctic like airmass impacting eastern NM during the 2nd aforementioned active period with the cold temperatures continuing through the end of the month due to extended periods of low clouds and freezing fog over the lingering snowpack. The wetter than average conditions for the month resulted in the removal of extreme drought across the San Francisco River Valley and removal of severe drought across almost all of southern Chaves County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought was removed from the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong arctic cold front moved across west Texas and southeast New Mexico during the afternoon and evening hours of the 23rd. Meanwhile, a potent upper-level trough neared the region from the west. Widespread rain began during the morning of the 23rd in western portions of the area and gradually transitioned eastward through the day. As the front moved across the area, rain transitioned to sleet and snow from north to south (with freezing rain being more common south of the Pecos River and in the Davis Mountains). Off and on bands of sleet and snow continued across the Permian Basin and southeast New Mexico on the 24th, and became mostly snow by the 25th before moving east of the area late in the afternoon. Snowfall/sleet totals reached winter storm criteria across much of the northern basin and in southeast New Mexico. Lower snow and ice totals were reported further south.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported a two-day storm total snow/sleet accumulation of 4.3 inches with a liquid equivalent of 0.89 inches. Light snow continued after the report came in. The total duration of the event is estimated by radar and the spotter's reports.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough moved east across the northern Rockies during the day. Strong mid and upper level winds over eastern New Mexico at the base of this trough along with deep daytime mixing resulted in brief high wind gusts at Bartley, Clines Corners, and the Tucumcari Airport. Winds tapered off during the mid evening hours as the disturbance exited east into the central United States.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Clines Corners ASOS measured a brief high wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong section of the jetstream between two upper level systems brought a brief period of high wind gusts to a portion of central NM January 8th. Peak gusts of 58 mph to 65 mph were measured by the New Mexico Ziamet station southeast of Vaughn, NM.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Ziamet mesonet station southeast of Vaughn, NM in southwestern Guadalupe County measured high wind gusts of 58 mph and 65 mph.
